tweets.text: Shouldn't we be focused on the whole supply demand of equities and if so drill down on each cohort.  So for the corporate cohort focus on all of their supply and demand actions.  For a decade they have been reducing share available to investors via buybacks and less issuance both primary and secondary.  There has been a sizable using any metric change in the past year and it's not going back any time soon.  Those focused on IPO's only are silly. Who are they if you don't mind me asking?

tweets.text:
If I told you the red line when adjust for ODTE and Leveraged ETF is double the reported number and growing rapidly  where the reported number needed no correction in 2000 would that surprise you? double that number would mean financial leverage was near or above peak vs market cap.  

Check the cost of box spreads and other equity specific financing to triangulate and perhaps a better synthesis than using simple unadjusted finra margin emerges. 

That said the obvious point of using finra margin without considering market cap is silly.  

One last comment on the topic is change.  The change even in the reported finra/mc is important as it decribes new leverage being added when asset prices are high

SK Hynix +57.9%

Our other HBM picks have also done quite well:

$ONTO +43.9%
Hanmi Semi +193% https://t.co/B4ECpEUwPK

tweets.text:
We’ve done less “In Conversation” articles recently as our audience expressed a preference for more thematic and analytical work. However, I really think there’s a lack of content that involves CEOs simply explaining what it is their company does, how they make money and what their long term vision is. 

Going back to read this conversation we had with the CEO of $OUST, it’s a really excellent resource for anyone who wants to get the story in their own words (as the stock has had a wildly volatile climb and quadrupled since we originally looked into it). 

Should we do some more of these sit downs with management?
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Shortly after adding $OUST to our robotics basket on May 16th, we had the pleasure of sitting down with CEO Angus Pacala to discuss all things LiDaR and Ouster’s path to profitability.

Since we first went long, OUST has risen a staggering 139%. 

https://t.co/oPZ0MCpBm8

QNX is the opt-in safety certification on the regulated IGX Thor path, while the mass-humanoid workhorse Jetson Thor defaults to Linux and ROS. The unit ramp is slower because the binding constraint is physical rather than software, namely actuators and China-gated

Memory companies (like micron) are to memory what debeers is to diamonds.

“Supply constrained” because they understand that memory is a commodity + cyclical, and their way to milk it this time around without future overhang would be through limiting expansion, propping up

tweets.text:
I'd tend to side with @jukan05 here:

- Power keeps the data centre alive
- Compute thinks
- Memory lets the agent remain itself

People are underestimating the potential of persistent memory; it's THE most important "sub-theme" coming out of the AI trade

Yes memory stocks (and now some altcoin memory tokens) have pumped, but that doesn't mean there isn't further to run

Pay attention to AI memory quality; to coin an Animal Farm quote:

"All memory is equal, but some memory is more equal than others"

I'll be dropping an article on AI memory in my free newsletter (link in my bio) so turn notis on + subscribe

Last article on AI inference will also be useful context, i've linked below
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Yes, MU is not Nvidia.

But going forward, it may become even more important than Nvidia.

Think about it. Inference is now directly tied to money. But inference does not get better simply by adding more Nvidia GPUs. In fact, GPUs are often underutilized in inference, sitting

tweets.text:
“Samsung and SKHynix to invest a trillion”
“Elon says production has to increase”
“Tim cook has never seen anything like it”

“That’s bullish” 

Quite the opposite. 

1) Capex increase 
2) Memory supply expansion 

If anything, extortion by capping supply on a commodity, debeers style, was their best bet. Although, they couldn’t have kept that going forever. 

1) Customers would invest in alternatives 
2) Competitors would arise 
3) Memory supply expansion anyway 

The tighter the bottleneck, the greater the incentive to widen it.

“That’s bullish” 

Quite the opposite. 

1) Capex increase 
2) Memory supply expansion 

If anything, extortion by capping supply on a commodity, debeers style, was their best bet. Although, they couldn’t have kept that going forever. 

1) Customers would invest in alternatives 
2) Competitors would arise 
3) Memory supply expansion anyway 

The tighter the bottleneck, the greater the incentive to widen it.

tweets.text:
$GM cuts 1,000 workers and replaces them with 50 robots (Jun 22nd report)

Reports also show GM is working on a deal with $NVDA on factory robotics.

This is industry validation for robotics, since it likely demonstrates increased opex margins + efficiency from employee automation. 

Especially from companies like $AMZN to General Motors with assembly/warehouse lines that have high headcount.

I think the technology is already here and use cases are already proven. Robotics/Humanoids are just pre-scale right now. 

The wording from large companies is “assistive robotics”… But in all honesty, it’s here to automate away the human workforce and increase profitability.

I don’t think companies want to be paying insurance, salaries, etc. when robots can do a better job, and 24/7, so very sad to see potential job losses in the future from robotics automation.

Whether people like it or not; seems like the inflection point for robotics is here.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Robotics is next. 

Both deal count and investment amounts are skyrocketing per pitchbook March data (source: a16z)

Good thing is: the same AI DC exposure often has cross-exposure to humanoid ramp.

Like DRAM/NAND with memory (on humanoid inference/storage) or DFB lasers with https://t.co/7BrzCd3cWD

Whether people like it or not; seems like the inflection point for robotics is here.

tweets.text:
Gm ☕️

Lots of growth and labour market data this week to look forward to before Friday — as per MMM regime modelling I think the most probable move is back towards Goldilocks from Overheat over coming months as inflation impulses have collapsed whilst growth impulses have bottomed (and despite the noise liquidity impulses have v-reversed from bottom decile to top decile over the past couple of months)

Writing up this week’s Market Outlook, which will be available shortly at @Ostium and then later on X and Substack

Will share more detailed thoughts in there 🤝

tweets.text:
Just some notes on $POET AGM and read through on optical markets:

- "The top three laser suppliers control 68% of the market, and they’re completely sold out for the next two years"

$LITE CEO said into 2028, so POET implicitly confirms laser shortage is going into 2029 now.

- NRE with a new customer, building on POET’s interposer for high-power external light source.

This is high confidence $SIVE as laser supplier given the Sept 29, 2025 PR on ELS, and new customer qualification would be material for revenue if it goes into volume ramp. 

- Poet expects Lumilens commercial agreement to scale to over $500 million over the next 5 years.

Lumilens claimed a top-3 hyperscaler was their initial customer (Linkedin OSINT)

- $830M cash on hand on balance sheet (this is more for Poet fundamentals).

- "The entire optical components industry today is facing a severe shortage of critical components."

Reaffirming what we know already regarding optical bottlenecks.

- "Production ramp in the second half of 2026 this year." 

Just production timelines H2. 

In terms of $POET volumes:
- existing capacity around 1 million optical engines per year
- projected demand exiting 2027 around 1 million optical engines per month
- roughly 10x capacity expansion

I don't own Poet, but if management delivers on these projections it directionally looks very positive.

However something to note is that unlike other suppliers that have named hyperscaler customers to to back up capacity revenues like $AAOI: $POET seems more questionable.

But this does look like a very positive outlook for $POET if they match projections.

- Scaling massive reusable rockets for orbital compute with $RKLB to $SPCX.

- Backflipping Boston Dynamics and Unitree humanoids to replace the human workforce

- Star Wars laser beams from $EOS.ASX to AI DC lasers like $LITE.

- and we get industry Quantum commercialization end of decade

This is kinda crazy to be an investor in this timeframe. Feels like every movie from the Star Wars Death Star to Skynet is coming to life.

What’s next?

https://t.co/smUPJ3xjGC

tweets.text:
the chinese labs really are relentless with accelerating open source ai. deepseek is doing everything it can (and succeeding) in maintaining parity with top models, large american companies are shifting to chinese models (coinbase, microsoft)

deepseek is newly funded with $8B warchest. zhipu is basically state funded, stock is up 15X off the back of GLM’s success 

the irony of china leading open source while america throttles the release of gpt 5.6 and fable 

ofc china’s goal isn’t to open source intelligence it’s to kill the american lab valuations and win on cheapest cost to access models. china has the most compute if they can solve the chip problem
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
DeepSeek is the GOAT. 🐳

They just published DSpark, a new speculative decoding method that boosts throughput by 51% to 400%.

They also open-sourced DeepSpec, the training framework behind it.

This is the real open AI. https://t.co/Gj4afGKviJ
